title: 2026 Minnesota gubernatorial election
description: Minnesota will elect its governor and lieutenant governor on November 3, 2026, with party primaries scheduled for August 11, 2026. The race will decide control of the state’s top executive office and shape policy on the budget, education, public safety, health care, and taxes. It is expected to feature Democratic-Farmer-Labor and Republican candidates, along with possible independent or third-party contenders, and the campaign is likely to center on continuity in state government versus calls for tax relief, public safety changes, and broader government reform. This profile is current as of April 1, 2026.

candidates.name: Brad Kohler
candidates.summary: Brad Kohler is an independent candidate for governor of Minnesota, a former UFC fighter, and a business owner. His campaign says it is focused on making Minnesota more affordable through lower taxes, reduced regulation, public safety, parental rights, school choice, government transparency, and local control.

summary_sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

career_history.title: Business owner
career_history.description: Ballotpedia identifies him as a businessman.

Healthcare.stance: Kohler says reliable healthcare should be part of a stronger Minnesota, but his campaign materials do not provide a detailed healthcare policy proposal.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor
Brad Kohler's Vision for a Stronger Minnesota

Economy.stance: Kohler says he would eliminate the state business income tax, reduce personal income taxes, lower property taxes, cap car and truck tabs at $99, and cut excessive red tape and regulations to support affordability and growth.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor
Brad Kohler's Vision for a Stronger Minnesota

Climate/Energy.stance: Kohler’s campaign emphasizes affordability and says he would lower costs through market-oriented reforms and reduced regulation, but it does not lay out a detailed climate or energy platform.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Reproductive Rights.stance: I did not find a clear, direct public statement from Kohler’s campaign materials on abortion or reproductive rights in the sources reviewed.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Immigration.stance: Kohler’s campaign website does not appear to lay out a detailed immigration platform.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Social Justice.stance: Kohler’s campaign emphasizes inclusion, fundamental freedoms, human rights, and opposition to discrimination, framing those values as part of his “Independence Way” message.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Education.stance: Kohler says he supports expanded vocational training, accessible childcare for working parents, greater parental input in education, and more focused learning environments.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Tech & AI.stance: Kohler says he supports using technology ethically to benefit Minnesotans, but his campaign language is general and does not specify AI regulation or investment proposals.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Election Reform.stance: Kohler says he supports fair processes, secure and accessible elections, transparency in government and campaigns, and lower barriers for independent voices and new ideas.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

Local Issues.stance: Kohler’s local-issues message centers on reducing the cost of living through lower taxes, lower property taxes, capped car tabs, reduced regulation, vocational training, childcare support, and more local control.

sources.title: Home | Brad Kohler | For MN Governor

candidates.donor_summary: Public campaign-finance information for Brad Kohler’s 2026 governor campaign appears limited in the sources reviewed. The available federal FEC committee page is for a separate congressional committee, so it does not provide a complete picture of fundraising for the governor race.
candidates.voting_summary: Brad Kohler does not have a legislative voting record because he has not held elected legislative office. Publicly available comparisons therefore rely on his campaign statements and prior candidacies rather than roll-call votes.

candidates.name: Mike Newcome
candidates.summary: Mike Newcome is an independent candidate for governor of Minnesota and a businessman and entrepreneur from White Bear Lake. Ballotpedia says he graduated from White Bear Lake High School and earned a bachelor's degree from the University of St. Thomas in 1992. MPR News reported on March 30, 2026 that he ended his campaign for governor.

sources.title: Mike Newcome for Minnesota

Economy.stance: Promotes a pro‑business economic approach focused on improving Minnesota's business climate by lowering the corporate tax burden with a sliding‑scale tax based on company size and infrastructure needs. He also advocates reform of Minnesota's paid family leave policy to reduce burdens on employers and supports eliminating unfunded county mandates to ease local business compliance.

candidates.name: Amy Klobuchar
candidates.summary: Amy Klobuchar is a senior U.S. senator from Minnesota and a Democratic-Farmer-Labor candidate for governor in 2026. Her campaign has emphasized affordability, public safety, and opposition to the Trump administration, drawing on her long Senate tenure and earlier service as Hennepin County attorney.

Economy.stance: Klobuchar says her campaign will focus on affordability and a competitive economy, with an emphasis on fiscal discipline, workforce development, trade, and infrastructure to support job growth.

Climate/Energy.stance: Klobuchar has a long-standing public record supporting climate and clean-energy policy, including environmental conservation and homegrown energy priorities. However, reliable sources located for her 2026 governor campaign did not show a detailed Minnesota-specific climate platform, so this stance reflects her broader Senate record rather than a campaign-specific plan.

Reproductive Rights.stance: Klobuchar strongly supports reproductive rights and abortion access, including codifying Roe v. Wade into law and protecting the right to travel for reproductive health care.

Foreign Policy.stance: Klobuchar has pushed for U.S. support for Ukraine, including bipartisan efforts to recover abducted Ukrainian children and to sanction Russia if it does not return them. She has also supported sanctions and allied assistance for Ukraine.

Social Justice.stance: Klobuchar has called for limiting aggressive federal immigration enforcement in Minnesota and for accountability and reform of ICE. Her public statements frame the issue as one of civil rights, due process, and lawful public safety policy.

Education.stance: Klobuchar says every child deserves a good education and supports fully funded schools, early education, STEM, apprenticeships, community and technical colleges, and college affordability.

Local Issues.stance: Klobuchar has focused on limiting aggressive federal immigration enforcement in Minnesota and on accountability and reform of ICE. She framed that position during her January 2026 governor bid launch and reiterated it in a February 2026 statement calling for ICE withdrawal from Minnesota and a full overhaul of the agency.

candidates.donor_summary: Amy Klobuchar’s fundraising is dominated by securities and investment interests, with additional support from lawyers and law firms, real estate, and media and entertainment donors. OpenSecrets shows her leadership PAC fundraising at about $2.0 million raised in the 2019–2024 period, with Centerbridge Partners among the top disclosed contributors.
candidates.voting_summary: Amy Klobuchar has an extensive U.S. Senate voting record. Her recorded votes should be read as Senate roll calls from her service as a senator, not as gubernatorial actions, and they show a long-running pattern of supporting Democratic caucus priorities.

candidates.name: Kendall Qualls
candidates.summary: Kendall Qualls is a Republican candidate for governor of Minnesota and a retired U.S. Army officer and former health care executive. His campaign emphasizes public safety, education reform, and conservative policy priorities.

career_history.title: Health care executive
career_history.description: Worked as a health care executive.

Healthcare.stance: Qualls has not released a detailed health-care policy platform in the sources reviewed. Available campaign materials emphasize his background as a health care executive rather than a specific set of health-care proposals.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home
About - Kendall Qualls for Governor

Economy.stance: Qualls proposes cutting personal income taxes, business taxes, property taxes, and state fees, and eliminating taxes on Social Security benefits. He says the plan would leave more money in Minnesotans’ pockets and support economic growth.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: 15 Commitments (Contract)
Kendall Qualls: A contract to save Minnesota

Climate/Energy.stance: Qualls favors market-driven, affordable energy policies and reduced regulatory burdens, emphasizing lower energy costs for Minnesotans rather than aggressive climate mandates.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home

Reproductive Rights.stance: Qualls identifies as pro-life. Reporting in early 2026 said he would focus on personal persuasion rather than new abortion legislation to reduce abortions in Minnesota, and he has also pledged to veto state funding for abortion.

sources.title: How Minnesota Republicans are recalibrating on abortion
Minnesota Republicans are recalibrating about abortion after past election losses

Immigration.stance: Qualls appears to favor stricter immigration enforcement and has publicly aligned with hardline Republican messaging on ICE and deportation of people in the country illegally.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home
Conservative Gubernatorial Candidate Kendall Qualls Doubles Down on ICE Support

Guns & Safety.stance: Qualls emphasizes public safety and mental health approaches while opposing broad gun-control measures aimed at law-abiding gun owners. He supports targeted safety efforts and enforcement and argues violence should be addressed by tackling underlying causes rather than restricting Second Amendment rights.

sources.title: Minnesota governor candidate talks with local gun owners about gun control policies

Foreign Policy.stance: I did not find a specific foreign policy platform from Qualls’s campaign materials or reliable reporting in the sources reviewed.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home

Social Justice.stance: Qualls has criticized diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives and promotes an anti-DEI stance in education and public institutions.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls hopes anti-DEI message will win him governor's race

Education.stance: Qualls supports expanding school choice, giving parents more input over curriculum, expanding vocational and technical training, and opposing ethnic studies and DEI-related curricula he says divide students.

sources.title: Republican governor candidates call for changes to Minnesota education system
Kendall Qualls enters 2026 Minnesota governor’s race as a GOP nomination contender
Contract - Kendall Qualls for Governor

Tech & AI.stance: Qualls has not published a formal AI or technology policy. He has discussed AI in campaign contexts, including questions about AI data centers and water-resource considerations, but no definitive policy has been released publicly.

sources.title: The Iron Range, Ethics, and the AI Frontier: Minnesota’s 2026 Crossroads
INTRODUCING Q&A WITH KENDALL QUALLS — AI data centers Q&A

Election Reform.stance: Qualls’ campaign materials reviewed here do not present a detailed election-reform agenda.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home
Caucus - Kendall Qualls for Governor

Local Issues.stance: Qualls says government should focus on safe streets, strong schools, and good jobs, and his campaign centers on cleaning up fraud and restoring order in Minnesota.

sources.title: Kendall Qualls for Governor: Home
Government should focus on safe streets, strong schools, and good jobs...

candidates.donor_summary: Public finance information for Qualls’ 2026 governor campaign was limited in the sources reviewed. FEC records confirm a prior federal candidate file, but I did not find a clear 2026 governor-race finance summary with top donors or total raised.
candidates.voting_summary: Kendall Qualls does not have a legislative voting record because he has not held elected legislative office. Vote Smart’s public profile shows no roll-call voting history and indicates he did not provide responses to the 2020 Political Courage Test.

candidates.name: Kristin Robbins
candidates.summary: Kristin Robbins is a Republican member of the Minnesota House of Representatives who represents District 37A and is running for governor in 2026. Her official House biography says she is an executive director and lists her education as a B.A. in economics and political science from Bethel University and an M.A. in economics from Washington University. Her campaign says it will focus on taxes, fraud prevention, public safety, schools, and opposition to sanctuary policies.

summary_sources.title: Rep. Kristin Robbins (37A) - Minnesota House of Representatives
State Rep. Kristin Robbins, west metro Republican, announces run for governor

voting_record.bill_description: Minnesota House roll call on voter registration and absentee voting provisions.

source.title: Roll Call: MN HF3 | 2023-2024 | 93rd Legislature - LegiScan

voting_record.bill_description: Bill to temporarily prohibit Metropolitan Council spending on new light rail transit projects.

source.title: Bill to halt new light rail projects stalls on House Floor - Session Daily

Healthcare.stance: Kristin Robbins supports limiting Minnesota's Paid Family and Medical Leave program, introducing bills to reduce regulatory burden on employers and provide an opt-out option for small businesses while preserving basic leave provisions.

sources.title: GOP gubernatorial candidate pushes for limits on Minnesota paid leave law
Statement from Chair Robbins on Paid Family & Medical Leave
Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Economy.stance: Proposes comprehensive tax relief to grow Minnesota’s economy: cap property tax growth at 3% (or inflation), phase down state income tax toward 4%, and eliminate taxes on Social Security; stop automatic gas tax increases and repeal the license-tab tax indexing; cut taxes on tips and overtime and cut the estate tax; all while pursuing an all-of-the-above energy plan to lower energy costs.

sources.title: Prosperity Plan - Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor
LIVE: Rep. Kristin Robbins lays out plan to make life more affordable for Minnesotans

Climate/Energy.stance: Robbins says she would lower energy costs through an “all of the above” energy policy, alongside reducing regulation and fees.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Reproductive Rights.stance: I did not find a clear, direct statement from Robbins on abortion or reproductive rights in the campaign materials reviewed.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Immigration.stance: Robbins says Minnesota should have “no sanctuary for illegal immigrants” and frames immigration through a law-and-order lens.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Guns & Safety.stance: Robbins has a record of opposing several gun-safety measures, including red flag laws and universal background checks in Minnesota in 2020 and 2023, and she opposed a 2025 bill adding Minnesota law enforcement to a nationwide gun-trace data-sharing system; NRA rated her highly. This suggests a more permissive stance on gun access, prioritizing public safety through enforcement rather than expansive gun-control measures.

Foreign Policy.stance: I did not find a specific foreign policy position in Robbins’s campaign materials.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Social Justice.stance: Robbins’ campaign messaging emphasizes public safety, parental rights, and opposition to what she calls “woke nonsense,” but the reviewed sources did not provide a detailed social-justice platform.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Education.stance: Robbins emphasizes focusing schools on achievement and parental involvement, and has advocated expanding K-12 education tax credits to include tuition and higher deduction amounts, signaling support for school choice options.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor
Rep. Robbins Introduces Bill to Expand Education Tax Credits

Tech & AI.stance: I did not find a specific AI or technology policy statement on Robbins' campaign site in the sources reviewed.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

Election Reform.stance: Robbins advocates strengthening fraud prevention and state oversight to safeguard elections and public programs, including creating an independent Office of Inspector General to regularly audit programs and enforce a 'no fraud, no excuses' culture; support for top-to-bottom audits of major programs (including Medicaid) to root out fraud and improve accountability.

sources.title: Minnesota Reformer: Preventing fraud a centerpiece of Rep. Kristin Robbins’ campaign for governor
Legislative Update from Rep. Kristin Robbins

Local Issues.stance: Robbins says she will cut taxes and fees, freeze property taxes, end the tax on Social Security benefits, lower energy costs, reduce regulation, support law enforcement, and oppose sanctuary policies.

sources.title: Kristin Robbins for Minnesota Governor

candidates.donor_summary: Kristin Robbins has a state-level campaign-finance footprint in Minnesota, with public donor records showing support from individual donors and some PAC or organizational giving. The sources reviewed do not provide a single consolidated total for her 2026 governor campaign, but they do show her as a tracked Minnesota candidate with public state finance disclosures.
candidates.voting_summary: Kristin Robbins has a legislative voting record as a Minnesota House member. Vote Smart provides key-vote pages for her, indicating recorded votes on issues such as health care and abortion; her broader roll-call pattern can be reviewed through those issue-specific summaries.

candidates.name: Lisa Demuth
candidates.summary: Lisa Demuth is the Republican speaker of the Minnesota House and represents House District 13A. She is running for governor in 2026 and has centered her campaign on taxes, spending, public safety, education, and energy policy.

summary_sources.title: Rep. Lisa Demuth (13A) - Minnesota House of Representatives

career_history.title: Speaker of the Minnesota House of Representatives

voting_record.bill_description: Minnesota House roll call on voter registration and absentee voting provisions.

source.title: Roll Call: MN HF3 | 2023-2024 | 93rd Legislature - LegiScan

voting_record.bill_description: Minnesota House bill to eliminate state taxes on tips and some overtime pay.

source.title: State taxes on tips and some overtime pay would be eliminated ... - Session Daily

Healthcare.stance: Demuth’s campaign site says she will focus on a broad set of priorities and includes a health-care section, but the homepage material reviewed here does not provide a detailed health-care policy.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor

Economy.stance: Lisa Demuth's top priority is helping Minnesota families afford their lives by cutting income taxes (proposing to reduce the lowest two tax brackets), dialing back car tab fees, and ending taxes on Social Security benefits; she also supports returning any state surplus dollars to taxpayers.

sources.title: Lisa's Priorities - Lisa Demuth for Governor
Republican governor candidate Lisa Demuth talks priorities during tour stops

Climate/Energy.stance: All-of-the-above energy policy that lowers energy costs for Minnesota families, repeals the state's nuclear energy ban, and reduces burdensome mandates to improve reliability and affordability of heating and electricity.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth's Priorities

Immigration.stance: Advocates collaboration with federal immigration authorities on ICE operations in Minnesota, supports detaining individuals here illegally who have criminal convictions in state custody, and opposes sanctuary policies; argues Minnesota should honor federal detention requests and work with federal partners to enhance public safety.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth urges Gov. Walz to collaborate with feds on ICE in Minnesota
Here's where Minnesota Republicans stand on immigration, Good and Pretti killings?

Guns & Safety.stance: Since the 2026 legislative session, Lisa Demuth has signaled willingness to consider gun-violence reduction measures in conjunction with a broader public-safety and mental-health framework, emphasizing bipartisanship and protection of Second Amendment rights, rather than pushing a standalone assault-weapons ban. She and House GOP Leader Harry Niska stated that gun-safety bills should be debated through the regular process, with focus on student safety, mental health access, and lawful gun ownership; they indicated openness to bipartisan legislation and not limiting topics solely to gun bans.

sources.title: MinnPost - Walz resuscitates push for assault weapons ban, but has enough changed since Annunciation?

Foreign Policy.stance: I did not find a specific foreign policy position in Demuth’s campaign materials.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor

Social Justice.stance: Demuth’s campaign messaging emphasizes public safety, opposition to what it describes as ideological priorities in schools, and a more conservative approach to governance.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor

Education.stance: Lisa Demuth's Education priority remains: Minnesota schools need a dramatic reset with a focus on academic excellence and reading proficiency, while pushing back on perceived indoctrination; her campaign emphasizes parental input and reducing education bureaucracy. There is no clear published policy shift as of now beyond reiterating the 'dramatic reset' and center on reading proficiency.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth priorities

Tech & AI.stance: I did not find a specific AI or technology policy statement from Demuth in the sources reviewed.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor

Election Reform.stance: Demuth’s campaign says she will fight fraud, clean house in the executive branch, and create a “No Fraud, No Excuses” culture with standardized internal controls and accountability.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor

Local Issues.stance: Demuth’s campaign prioritizes lower taxes, lower energy costs, reduced regulation, stronger schools, and support for law enforcement.

sources.title: Lisa Demuth for Governor
I'm excited to be at the Wright County Republican gubernatorial candidate forum...

candidates.donor_summary: Lisa Demuth’s campaign finance activity is tracked in Minnesota state records, and her donor profile is available through FollowTheMoney. The available records show disclosed contributions tied to her legislative and campaign activity, but the reviewed sources do not provide a single consolidated total for the 2026 governor race.
candidates.voting_summary: Lisa Demuth has a Minnesota House voting record dating to 2019. Vote Smart provides her key votes and legislative record, which can be used to review her positions on issues such as campaign finance, executive branch matters, and other state legislation.

candidates.name: Peggy Bennett
candidates.summary: Peggy Bennett is a Republican member of the Minnesota House of Representatives from Albert Lea and a candidate for governor of Minnesota in 2026. Before entering politics, she taught elementary school in Albert Lea for 33 years and has centered her campaign on affordability, education, public safety, and government reform.

summary_sources.title: Rep. Peggy Bennett (23A) - Minnesota House of Representatives
About Peggy – Peggy Bennett for Governor
Rep. Peggy Bennett announces run for Minnesota Governor - KTTC

Healthcare.stance: Support aggressive healthcare reform to lower costs and improve access by pursuing federal waivers to enable innovative coverage and payment models, expanding insurance plan choices and larger pools. In Minnesota, promote direct contracting and Direct Primary Care, reduce burdens on clinicians (licensing and prior authorization), and pursue efficiency measures to curb waste and expand access, including reforms to Medicaid oversight.

Economy.stance: Peggy Bennett's 'Affordable Living' platform outlines an economy policy framework centered on reducing regulatory burdens and unfunded mandates, eliminating Minnesota's tax on Social Security, pursuing pro-growth tax reform to improve competitiveness, and addressing cost-of-living pressures (housing, energy, childcare) to boost affordability and economic opportunity across the state.

sources.title: Why I'm Running – Peggy Bennett for Governor
Affordable Living – Peggy Bennett for Governor

Climate/Energy.stance: No explicit climate/energy policy proposal found on Peggy Bennett's campaign site; her materials emphasize government efficiency, affordable living, and education; no clear energy or climate policy stance publicly published.

sources.title: Peggy Bennett for Governor – People Before Politics

Reproductive Rights.stance: Bennett says she is pro-life, opposes abortion, and would sign any “reasonable” bill that helps save babies and support women facing unplanned pregnancies.

sources.title: Other Topics – Peggy Bennett for Governor
Peggy Bennett for Governor – People Before Politics

Immigration.stance: Bennett has said the federal government has the right and duty to enforce legal immigration, and her campaign messaging supports removing criminals who are in the country illegally.

sources.title: Legislative News from Rep. Peggy Bennett
Peggy Bennett for Governor – People Before Politics

Guns & Safety.stance: Peggy Bennett endorses stricter enforcement on gun crime: increased minimum sentences for repeat gun offenders, no cashless bail when a gun is used in a crime, and stronger accountability for judges and prosecutors who go soft on gun crimes; she argues for prioritizing public safety and better enforcement over new gun restrictions on law-abiding gun owners.

sources.title: Public Safety – Peggy Bennett for Governor

Foreign Policy.stance: Peggy Bennett supports coordinating with federal immigration authorities to enforce federal immigration law in Minnesota, including targeted removal of criminal illegal immigrants and opposition to sanctuary policies; she argues the federal government has the right and duty to enforce legal immigration.

Education.stance: Prioritize improving student reading and core academics by implementing Science of Reading-based instruction, increasing parental transparency and local school autonomy, and pursuing targeted discipline reforms to reduce classroom chaos; supports fixes to school policies (including SROs) to keep students safe and focused on learning.

She also advocates reducing property taxes by ending unfunded state mandates and promoting transparent, single-subject governance.

candidates.donor_summary: Peggy Bennett’s publicly disclosed fundraising is reflected in Minnesota campaign-finance records and donor-tracking databases, including FollowTheMoney and OpenSecrets. The available records show donor activity tied to her legislative and campaign committees, but not a single consolidated total for the 2026 governor race.
candidates.voting_summary: Peggy Bennett has a recorded voting history as a Minnesota House member, with issue-specific roll calls and legislative materials available for review. Her public record can be evaluated through Vote Smart and Minnesota legislative sources, though the draft profile’s two cited entries show absences rather than affirmative votes.

candidates.summary: Kristin Robbins is a Republican member of the Minnesota House of Representatives from Maple Grove and the Republican nominee for governor in 2026. She has served in the House since 2019 and chairs the Fraud Prevention and State Agency Oversight Committee while also serving on taxes and higher education.

flags.concern: The entry named 'updated_utc' is a system timestamp metadata field, not a candidate. It has been incorrectly inserted into the candidates array at index 9, corrupting the data structure. This object contains mixed/confused content with some Kristin Robbins information alongside generic stance placeholders.
flags.suggestion: Remove this entire object from the candidates array immediately. The proper timestamp already exists at the root level as 'updated_utc': '2026-04-01T05:36:23.737273+00:00'. Verify that Kristin Robbins (index 5) is the authoritative candidate profile and consolidate any missing information there.

flags.concern: Tim Walz is listed with 'incumbent: true' and the summary uses present-tense language ('will run for reelection,' 'bring forward a DFL ticket'). However, multiple external reviews explicitly flag that sources indicate 'Walz has dropped his reelection bid.' This is a fundamental factual contradiction that creates immediate credibility damage and makes the profile internally incoherent.
flags.suggestion: Verify Walz's actual candidacy status through authoritative sources (governor.state.mn.us, AP News, Star Tribune). If he withdrew (as reviews suggest), set 'incumbent: false', revise the summary to past tense ('Walz initially announced a reelection campaign but withdrew on [date]'), and add clear language about his exit. If he is still running, correct or remove all sources indicating withdrawal.

flags.concern: Mike Newcome's profile documents his campaign suspension in multiple issue sections ('As of March 30, 2026, Mike Newcome has suspended his gubernatorial campaign') and in the summary, yet he remains listed as an active candidate in the array without any status indicator or flag. This creates ambiguity about whether the profile describes a current or historical race state and whether readers should treat him as a viable contender.
flags.suggestion: Add a 'status' field to Newcome's candidate object with value 'withdrawn' and a 'withdrawn_date': '2026-03-30'. Alternatively, if the profile is intended to reflect only active candidates at a given reference date, remove Newcome entirely and document his withdrawal in a 'notable_events' or similar section. Do not leave readers uncertain about his actual candidacy.

flags.concern: The candidates array contains 10 entries, but reviews flag that Kristin Robbins appears to be duplicated or confused: index 5 has her full profile, and index 9 (the malformed 'updated_utc' entry) contains some Robbins-related information mixed with other candidates' policy stances. This creates ambiguity about the true roster and whether information is consolidated or scattered.
flags.suggestion: Audit the entire candidates array to ensure each unique candidate appears exactly once. Remove index 9 entirely. Verify that index 5 (Kristin Robbins' main profile) contains all her information. Cross-reference any Robbins-related content in index 9 and consolidate it into index 5.

flags.concern: Two Emerson College polls dated 2026-02-11 and 2026-03-24 have generic matchups ('Democratic candidates' vs 'Republican candidates') with null percentages for both. Additionally, there are two separate entries for 2026-03-24 that appear to be duplicates. These entries provide no actionable information and suggest either incomplete data collection, accidental duplication, or placeholder content that was never filled in.
flags.suggestion: Retrieve the actual polling percentages from the cited Emerson College source (emersoncollegepolling.com/minnesota-2026-poll-democrats-lead-gop-as-voters-cite-threats-to-democracy/) and populate with specific candidate matchups and percentages. Remove duplicate 2026-03-24 entries, retaining only one. If the specific generic-candidate breakdown data is unavailable, remove these entries and add a note to 'polling_note' explaining the limitation.

flags.concern: Several voting record entries have dates and attributions that warrant verification. For example, H.R. 8 (Bipartisan Background Checks Act) is dated '2013-01-01', but this bill was voted on in 2012. Additionally, Tim Walz served in Congress from 2007–2019, and some vote dates fall outside or at the margins of this window. Inaccurate voting record dates undermine trust in factual rigor.
flags.suggestion: Verify all voting record entries against Congress.gov official Senate/House roll-call records. Correct dates to match actual vote dates. Add a clarifying note in the 'voting_summary' field that these are Walz's House voting records from his 2007–2019 service (if he is still a candidate) or that they represent his historical legislative record (if he has withdrawn from the 2026 race).

flags.concern: The source URL for Klobuchar's vote on 'S.5 Laken Riley Act' is 'https://www.congress.gov/bill/118th-congress/senate-bill/5', which points to a Senate bill page. However, the source_type is labeled 'government' without clarification that this is a Senate vote record. The URL should ideally point to the actual Senate roll-call vote record for clarity.
flags.suggestion: Update the source URL to point directly to the Senate's official voting record for S.5 (e.g., 'https://www.senate.gov/cgi-bin/vote-display.cfm...' or Congress.gov's Senate roll-call page). Alternatively, add 'source_chamber: "Senate"' to clarify the voting body.

flags.concern: The source title is 'Lisa Demuth - Minnesota Legislative Scorecard - Freedom Index', which is a clear copy-paste error. The entry is supposed to document Peggy Bennett's voting record, not Lisa Demuth's.
flags.suggestion: Correct the source title to reference Peggy Bennett's voting record (e.g., 'Peggy Bennett - Minnesota Legislative Scorecard - Freedom Index'). Verify the source URL points to Bennett's record, not Demuth's.

flags.concern: Nearly all candidates (Walz, Kohler, Newcome, Qualls, Robbins, Demuth, Bennett) have empty education arrays despite being public figures with documented academic backgrounds. Only Amy Klobuchar's education is populated (Yale BA, University of Chicago Law School JD). This creates significant asymmetry and reduces profile completeness and credibility.
flags.suggestion: Systematically research education for all candidates using official legislative biographies, Ballotpedia, campaign websites, and reputable news profiles. For example: Kristin Robbins is documented on Ballotpedia as having a B.A. in economics and political science (Bethel University) and M.A. in economics (Washington University); this information should be added to her education array. If no public education information is verifiable for a candidate, explicitly note '"education": [{"note": "No public education information found"}]' rather than leaving the array empty.

flags.concern: Brad Kohler's career_history is minimal ('Mixed martial artist / UFC fighter' 1997–2001, 'Private business' 2000–null). This is sparse for a gubernatorial candidate, and the business entry lacks specificity (type, size, industries, role).
flags.suggestion: Research and expand career_history with more detail: What type of business does/did he own? How long did it operate? What industries? Use campaign website, news profiles, and Ballotpedia. Also populate the empty social_media object with verified campaign social media URLs (Facebook, Twitter/X, etc.).

flags.concern: Brad Kohler's social_media object is empty ({}), despite him being an active gubernatorial candidate likely to have campaign social presence.
flags.suggestion: Research and populate with verified campaign social media URLs (Facebook, Twitter/X, Instagram, etc.). If no public accounts exist, note explicitly (e.g., '"social_media": {"note": "No public campaign social media accounts found"}').

flags.concern: Mike Newcome's career_history array is empty despite the summary describing him as a 'businessman and entrepreneur from White Bear Lake.' Education details (University of St. Thomas BA 1992) are mentioned in the summary but not in the structured education array.
flags.suggestion: Populate career_history with specific business ventures (company names, titles, industries, years). Move education information from the narrative summary into the structured education array with proper fields (institution, degree, field, year).

flags.concern: Kendall Qualls is described as a 'retired U.S. Army officer and former health care executive,' but career_history lacks critical details: military rank, branch of service, years of service, and names of health care organizations with job titles.
flags.suggestion: Research and populate career_history with: (a) Army service: branch, rank, years; (b) Health care roles: organization names, job titles, years. These are standard biographical elements for major candidates and are crucial for voter evaluation.

flags.concern: Kendall Qualls' education array is empty, lacking documented academic credentials for a major Republican gubernatorial candidate.
flags.suggestion: Research education details from Ballotpedia, campaign site (kqformn.com), or reputable news profiles and populate the array.

flags.concern: Kristin Robbins, Lisa Demuth, and Peggy Bennett (all sitting legislators) have empty education arrays, reducing profile completeness. Robbins' Ballotpedia page documents her education; Demuth and Bennett also have documented academic backgrounds.
flags.suggestion: For Robbins: Add B.A. in economics and political science (Bethel University, documented on Ballotpedia) and M.A. in economics (Washington University). For Demuth and Bennett: Research and populate from official House biographies, Ballotpedia, or campaign sites.

flags.concern: Amy Klobuchar's Climate/Energy stance is marked 'medium' confidence with a note that no detailed Minnesota-specific climate platform was found for her 2026 governor campaign. While acknowledging this gap is appropriate, the sourcing relies heavily on her Senate record rather than campaign-specific proposals.
flags.suggestion: If no detailed 2026 climate platform exists after thorough search, the 'medium' confidence is justified. However, document the search effort: did you check her campaign website, news coverage of campaign stops, debate transcripts, or official campaign documents? If such sources exist with detailed state-level climate commitments, add them. The current stance fairly reflects what is publicly available, but note that it is Senate-based rather than campaign-specific.

flags.concern: Lisa Demuth's Reproductive Rights stance field is empty (only empty string and 'low' confidence), which is a notable gap given abortion's prominence in Minnesota gubernatorial politics. This is a material issue for a major candidate and House Speaker.
flags.suggestion: Research Demuth's stance on abortion and reproductive rights through: (a) news coverage of her 2026 campaign; (b) legislative voting record on abortion-related bills; (c) official statements; (d) debate or forum appearances. Even if her position is not extensively publicized, document what is publicly available. The current blank is unacceptable for a frontrunner candidate.

flags.concern: Lisa Demuth's website field is null, yet lisaformn.com is extensively referenced throughout her issues section (Economy, Climate/Energy, Education, Election Reform, Local Issues). This is inconsistent and should be corrected.
flags.suggestion: Add 'website': 'https://lisaformn.com/' to Demuth's profile. This URL is verified through multiple references in her issue stances.

flags.concern: Lisa Demuth's image_url is null despite being the House Speaker and a frontrunner candidate. This reduces accessibility and visual identification in the profile.
flags.suggestion: Source an image URL from Wikimedia Commons (search 'Lisa Demuth Minnesota') or verify her campaign website hosts a photo and link accordingly.

flags.concern: Peggy Bennett's website field is null, yet peggybennettforgovernor.com is extensively cited in her issue sections (Healthcare, Economy, Education, Public Safety, etc.). This inconsistency should be resolved.
flags.suggestion: Add 'website': 'https://peggybennettforgovernor.com/' to Bennett's profile.

flags.concern: Peggy Bennett's image_url is null despite being a sitting legislator and gubernatorial candidate.
flags.suggestion: Source an image URL from Wikimedia Commons (search 'Peggy Bennett Minnesota') or campaign website.

flags.concern: Most candidates (Kohler, Newcome, Qualls, Robbins, Demuth, Bennett) have empty social_media objects ({}), suggesting incomplete data collection. Modern gubernatorial campaigns typically maintain visible social media presence.
flags.suggestion: Research and populate social media URLs for all candidates (Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn if applicable). Example: Kendall Qualls appears to have a Facebook presence (referenced in his issues sections). If a candidate has no verifiable public social media account, explicitly note that (e.g., '"social_media": {"note": "No public campaign social media accounts found"}') rather than leaving the object empty.

flags.concern: The polling section includes only three Emerson College entries, with two containing null data. There is no coverage from other major Minnesota or national pollsters (SurveyUSA, Morning Consult, Star Tribune/MPR partnership, university polls). For a major gubernatorial race in April 2026 (after the January announcement), this appears incomplete.
flags.suggestion: Search news archives, polling aggregators (FiveThirtyEight, RealClearPolitics), and local Minnesota news outlets for additional polls from February–April 2026. Include results from at least 2–3 additional reputable sources beyond Emerson. If few polls exist at this stage, note that explicitly in 'polling_note' (already added, which is good).

flags.concern: The generator array lists model identifiers that do not match standard naming conventions: 'gpt-5.4-mini' does not exist in OpenAI's actual model lineup (which uses gpt-4o, gpt-4-turbo, gpt-3.5-turbo, etc., not version 5.4). This raises questions about data provenance and whether the profile is synthetic test data or inaccurately attributed.
flags.suggestion: Correct model names to match actual, verifiable identifiers (e.g., 'gpt-4o', 'claude-3.5-haiku', 'gemini-2.0-flash'). If this profile is synthetic or test data, clearly label it as such in metadata. If model names are intentionally anonymized or represent custom fine-tunes, document that reasoning.

flags.concern: The profile's timestamp ('updated_utc': '2026-04-01T05:36:23.737273+00:00') is dated April 1, 2026, which is described as 'current as of April 1, 2026' in the race description. However, the profile documents events (Newcome withdrawal on March 30, polling from March 24) that are very recent. Some language (particularly for Walz) uses future tense ('will run for reelection') for a race happening months away, creating temporal inconsistency.
flags.suggestion: Clarify the profile's intended reference date and ensure all candidate statuses, polling dates, and tense consistency align with it. If the profile is meant to be 'as of April 1, 2026,' all candidate candidacy status should be finalized and clearly stated as of that date. Consider adding a 'reference_date' field at the root level for clarity.

reviews.summary: This profile demonstrates substantial research effort with generally credible sources (official campaign websites, Ballotpedia, reputable news outlets) and well-documented issue positions for most candidates. The issue stances themselves—particularly for Amy Klobuchar, Kristin Robbins, Lisa Demuth, and Peggy Bennett—are detailed, multi-sourced, and appear to accurately reflect each candidate's public record. However, the profile suffers from critical structural corruption and factual inconsistencies that severely undermine its reliability: (1) a system metadata field ('updated_utc') is incorrectly embedded as a candidate object in the array; (2) Tim Walz's incumbent status contradicts multiple sources indicating his withdrawal; (3) Mike Newcome's withdrawn status is documented in issue sections but not flagged in the candidate listing; (4) duplicate/confused candidate representation with Kristin Robbins appearing twice; (5) widespread gaps in candidate education and career history; (6) polling data contains null values with no explanation; and (7) attribution errors in voting records. These data integrity issues must be resolved before the profile can be trusted as an authoritative reference, even though the underlying policy research is solid.

flags.suggestion: Use accurate, standard identifiers for the generative models used.

reviews.summary: While the profile provides extensive issue coverage and cites credible sources for most candidates, it suffers from critical structural corruption and factual inconsistencies. Key issues include the erroneous inclusion of a system timestamp as a candidate entry, duplicate and conflicting candidate data for Kristin Robbins, and a failure to reconcile the 'incumbent' status of Tim Walz with reports of his withdrawal. Additionally, many biographical sections (education/career) are inexplicably empty for prominent legislators, and several polling entries contain null data, undermining the profile's professional reliability.

flags.concern: The entry for 'updated_utc' is a timestamp metadata field incorrectly placed in the candidates array, corrupting the structure and introducing non-candidate content.
flags.suggestion: Remove this entire object from the candidates array to restore proper formatting.

flags.concern: The profile lists Tim Walz as incumbent and seeking reelection, but sources and reviews indicate he has withdrawn, creating a factual inconsistency that misrepresents his status.
flags.suggestion: Update to 'incumbent: false', revise the summary to reflect his withdrawal with the correct date, and ensure all related fields are consistent with verified sources.

flags.concern: Mike Newcome is listed as a candidate despite multiple sources stating he suspended his campaign on March 30, 2026, leading to confusion about his active participation.
flags.suggestion: Add a 'status' field (e.g., 'status: "withdrawn", withdrawn_date: "2026-03-30") or remove him from the candidates array, and update the summary accordingly.

flags.concern: Percentages are set to [null, null], making this polling entry incomplete and uninformative, which reduces the profile's reliability on current race dynamics.

flags.concern: The education array for Tim Walz is empty, omitting key biographical details despite his public profile as a former congressman and governor.
flags.suggestion: Research and add verified education information (e.g., Chadron State College) from credible sources like Ballotpedia or official bios.

flags.concern: Brad Kohler's education array is empty, leaving a gap in his background despite available public information.
flags.suggestion: Populate with verified details if available, or note the absence explicitly.

flags.concern: Amy Klobuchar's voting record includes an 'absent' vote for a bill dated 2026-03-19, which is a future date relative to the profile's update timestamp, potentially indicating an error.
flags.suggestion: Verify all voting record dates against official sources like Congress.gov and correct or remove entries that do not align with her Senate service timeline.

flags.concern: Kendall Qualls' education array is empty, which is a notable omission for a candidate with a military and executive background.
flags.suggestion: Add verified education details from sources like Ballotpedia.

flags.concern: Kristin Robbins' education array is empty, despite her bio mentioning degrees from Bethel University and Washington University.
flags.suggestion: Populate with accurate details from her official House biography or other credible sources.

flags.concern: Lisa Demuth's education array is empty, reducing the completeness of her profile as a key Republican candidate.

flags.concern: Peggy Bennett's education array is empty, which limits the depth of her biographical information.

flags.concern: The listed models (e.g., 'gpt-5.4-mini') do not match standard naming conventions, raising questions about the profile's generation transparency and potential inaccuracies.
flags.suggestion: Update to accurate, verifiable model identifiers (e.g., 'gpt-4o') to improve credibility.

reviews.summary: This profile shows commendable effort in compiling detailed issue stances and sourcing for several candidates, such as Amy Klobuchar and Kristin Robbins, with high-confidence citations from official sites and reputable news outlets. However, it requires revisions due to structural errors, like a non-candidate entry in the candidates array, inconsistencies in candidate status (e.g., Tim Walz's withdrawal), and gaps in biographical details such as education and career history for many candidates, which affect overall completeness and accuracy.

polling_note: Polling data in this profile is limited and includes duplicate Emerson College entries; two March 24, 2026 entries contain placeholder null percentages and should be treated as incomplete until verified or replaced.
